body: Conducting a collaborative research since 2012, Maria Andersson and Nancy Atakan study Turkey’s integration of Swedish physical culture practices as part of the modernization efforts in the early 20th century. Investigating the adaptation of gymnastics techniques and folkloric elements, the artists unfold narratives on Selim Sırrı Tarcan, the founder of physical education and the Olympics Committee in Turkey, as well as on the accomplishments of his daughters.

Wooden bars, used by athletes for various warm-up exercises and performances, serve as the base for the exhibition furniture; canvas prints of the main text, drawings by artists, and videos displayed on different types of screens are placed on scaled versions of these wooden bars.
The adaptation of Maria Andersson's work Anthem as an interactive book format is also installed in a circular arrangement on the columns.

In this context, the Forum at Salt Beyoğlu is transformed into a gymnasium, incorporating dashed lines on both the floor and surrounding walls, alongside wooden stretching bars and hanging rings. As soon as visitors step into the structure, they find themselves in the gymnasium and hear the Youth Anthem coming from the nearest video piece.

Are pedestals or display cases only meant to hold artworks? Does their life end when the exhibition closes, or can they assume different roles with each use—becoming both support structures and design objects in their own right? Tracing display units produced for SALT's programs and repurposed on various occasions, Trash explores material decisions within exhibition-making processes. It offers a multilayered perspective on scenography and design practices through objects left behind from past exhibitions. Drawing on the institution’s 15-year history, the program opens up a discussion on the circulation of materials from gallery space to storage, the often-invisible exhibition infrastructures, and diverse forms of reuse.

These materials—neither the output of an industrial production line nor readily defined as “waste”—may gradually be written off and come to be considered “trash” while awaiting new functions in storage. Taking this nuance between waste and trash as a point of departure, the program underscores that value is not inherent in the material itself, but rather emerges from the context in which it is used and the meanings attributed to it. By assembling a pile of materials, the installation sets a stage that reveals the transience and mutability of this context, while also highlighting the exhibitionary conventions shaped by institutional decisions.

Transforming SALT Beyoğlu’s ground floor into a production space, where materials such as wood, glass, plexiglass, metal, foam, and paper are sorted, modified, and repurposed, Trash shows that discarded materials also carry memory and can hold archival qualities. Accompanied by workshops and talks on exhibition design practices, it revisits existing productions from new perspectives to develop proposals in dialogue with the space.

QR codes embedded within the materials connected visitors to SALT’s fifteen-year exhibition history and the exhibitions from which the materials originated, revealing the design logics, production methods, and material histories embedded within the institution’s exhibition practice.

Throughout the program, materials were transformed into new display structures, generating discussions on adaptive reuse within exhibition-making. In parallel, public talks with Ippolito Pestellini Laparelli, Carlos Mínguez Carrasco, Superpool, Can and Aslı Altay, and Aslı Çiçek broadened the discussion around exhibition design and scenography through different spatial and curatorial practices.

As Trash did not seek a final product, the installation itself remained intentionally open-ended. To sustain this ongoing discussion, Salt aims to build a network with the architecture and industrial design departments of the universities, developing structured routes for the material circulation and reuse of remnants generated by future projects. This vision found its first tangible expression on the final day of the program, which concluded with Aslı Çiçek’s talk: visitors claimed many of the raw materials and exhibition units that remained unused after the workshops, marking the first actual step toward a future of collective material sharing.

-- 
Program: Emirhan Altuner
Advisor: Can Altay
Workshop Facilitator and Advisor: Ahmet Sertaç Öztürk
Editor: Ezgi Yurteri
Program Assistants: Fulya Aras, Ayşe Bıçak
Installation: OCD Museum Works, 3T  Reklam

Originally presented at SALT in Istanbul, The 90s Onstage was reconfigured for its presentation at Kunstverein in Hamburg. Focusing on the notions of stage and performance, the exhibition brings together artistic productions from 1990s Turkey through archival materials, photographs, video works, and ephemera.

Retaining one of the defining elements of the original exhibition, this iteration unfolds around an elongated stage that extends throughout the gallery as a ramp, display surface, and viewing platform.

Each section is conceived as a compact installation composed of archival documents, artworks, slide projectors, CRT monitors, 4:3 video works, and subtle light interventions. At the center of this constellation, the stage-ramp emerges as a connective element, inviting visitors to re-enter the performances, encounters, and collective experiences that shaped 1990s Turkey.

---
Kunstverein in Hamburg: Milan Ther, Linda Epp, Martin Karcher

Program: Amira Akbıyıkoğlu
Research: Mine Söyler
Exhibition Design: Emirhan Altuner

Based on extensive research by artist Mike Bode and screenwriter Caner Yalçın, the exhibition exploring the fragmented history of Dark World, the 1953 film by Metin Erksan and Bedri Rahmi Eyüboğlu centered on the life of Âşık Veysel. Through archival materials, censored footage, altered scenes, and newly discovered film fragments, the exhibition examines how state ideology, censorship, and production processes reshaped the film’s narrative. Rather than reconstructing the “original” version, the project embraces the film’s fragmented condition to investigate the mechanisms of image-making, historical memory, and cultural representation in 1950s Turkey.

A hand-crafted animation constructs the exhibition’s visual identity through fragmented letters. Printed on transparent acetate sheets and assembled within a small slide viewer, the animation draws from the material qualities of film. The grain of the image, darkened edges, and isolated letterforms allude to the processes of editing and montage that underpin the exhibition’s investigation.

The minimal layout presents selected fragments from the film. One side of the space features sequences reflecting 1950s Turkey—scenes that were added during the censorship process and were not part of the film’s original version—alongside newly restored footage from the recently discovered print. All sequences remain silent, while the space is intermittently filled with the voice of Âşık Veysel in CRT monitors.

The pre-censored cut and the premiered version are still unknown, while the copies in circulation differ somewhat in sound, image, and editing. Adding to this fragmented legacy is a 35mm nitrate print, recently discovered in the archive of Mimar Sinan Fine Arts University Prof. Sami Şekeroğlu Cinema and Television Center, which includes footage unearthed for the first time.

The opposite side operates as a research laboratory, where the film’s original 1953 poster, a painting by Bedri Rahmi Eyüboğlu, censorship reports, and interviews with the researchers behind Dark World are assembled alongside the film’s final sequences unfolding in the background.

Screenwriter Caner Yalçın reconstructed and transcribed the multiple existing versions of Dark World. Printed materials displayed on a large central table reveal the differences between the scripts, while surrounding surfaces present archival material related to the film.

---

Notes on Air is an immersive installation conceived and designed by the Milan-based interdisciplinary design agency 2050+ for Salt Beyoğlu’s Forum. It stages a reimagination of air toxicity, employing a blend of material, sonic, and visual experiments. Through this aesthetic exploration, elements that are typically intangible—untouched, unheard, unseen—provide an instance for an embodied, sensorial encounter.

The installation encourages visitors to engage with the materiality of air as it unfolds through a series of interventions: curtains that reveal the chemical composition of air, each depicted with a color indicating different degrees of toxicity, alongside embroidered data and imagery demonstrating their prevalence across varying scales; a soundscape that conjures the otherwise inaudible conditions resulting from chemical reactions triggered by the interaction of these airborne elements; and an animated reconstruction of the Istanbul sky, crafted through photogrammetry, a technique typically used to capture static, solid objects. In this instance, it attempts to evidence and give form to the invisible gaseous substances that envelop the Earth.

---
2050+ Team: Che Facchin, Erica Petrillo, Guglielmo Campeggi, Ippolito Pestellini Laparelli, Kamil Dalkir, Meera Badran, Nils Grootenzerink, Sara Barbini

An exhibition exploring performance culture in Turkey from the late 1980s to the early 2000s through archival materials, video works, objects, and ephemera. Tracing performances across clubs, parks, historical landmarks, and abandoned sites, the exhibition reconsiders the stage as a temporary space of encounter and collective experience.

The exhibition design invites visitors onto the stage from the moment they enter. Built from reused partition walls and covered in yellow-painted vinyl, an elongated ramp carries the rhythm of İstiklal Avenue into the gallery. Acting as a stage, display surface, and circulation route, it shows video works and archival materials linked to the street’s role as one of Istanbul’s busiest sites for performance and public life.

Seretonin I (1989) and Seretonin II (1992) turned industrial buildings awaiting redevelopment into temporary stages for artistic production. These exhibitions brought together installations, performances, music, and happenings. They activated spaces like Feshane and Yedikule Gashouse while encouraging interactions between artists, workers, and audiences. Organized collectively and open to participation, Seretonin became a key part of Istanbul’s alternative cultural scene in the 1990s. Drawing from these industrial settings, the exhibition design recreates the factory as a performance space on the upper floors. Metal structures create usable volumes that visitors can explore, while 4:3 video works are shown on CRT monitors hung at different heights, reflecting the spatial and material conditions of the original venues.

Founded and directed by Hüseyin Katırcıoğlu, the Assos Performing Arts Festival transformed the village of Behramkale and the ancient city of Assos into a stage throughout the second half of the 1990s. Drawing from the festival’s relationship with the landscape, photographs are distributed throughout the gallery at varying heights. Echoing the movement of artists and audiences across Assos, the images extend beyond a conventional display format and spread through the space as a dispersed visual field.


The section culminates with a series of movable costumes designed by Selçuk Gürışık for SALT. Referencing the costumes he created for the festival, these performative objects bring a bodily presence into an exhibition otherwise composed of archival traces and documentation.

---

Originally presented at SALT in Istanbul, The 90s Onstage was reconfigured for its presentation at Kunstverein in Hamburg. Focusing on the notions of stage and performance, the exhibition brings together artistic productions from 1990s Turkey through archival materials, photographs, video works, and ephemera.

Each section is conceived as a compact installation composed of archival documents, artworks, slide projectors, CRT monitors, 4:3 video works, and subtle light interventions. At the center of this constellation, the stage-ramp emerges as a connective element, inviting visitors to re-enter the performances, encounters, and collective experiences that shaped 1990s Turkey.

Retaining one of the defining elements of the original exhibition, this iteration unfolds around an elongated stage that extends throughout the gallery as a ramp, display surface, and viewing platform.

Commissioned by SALT, CLIMAVORE: Seasons Made to Drift by Cooking Sections (Daniel Fernández Pascual and Alon Schwabe) explores how climate change reshapes food infrastructures, eating habits, and ecological relations. Through installations, archival materials, and spatial interventions, the exhibition traces emerging “seasons” shaped by drought, shifting ecosystems, industrial agriculture, and environmental transformation across Turkey and the Eastern Mediterranean.

Through selected cases, CLIMAVORE: Seasons Made to Drift studies and demonstrates instances that help identify new seasons in action. Exhausted (2021) undoes the tales of soil and human fertility from the Neolithic age in the so-called Fertile Crescent, to explore the crisis of infertility and the subsequent expansion of IVF facilities and treatments in contemporary Istanbul.

The exhibition starts with one of five commissioned works, Weathered (2021) on the ground floor of SALT Beyoğlu, where the Forum becomes a “prosthetic” forest of material records of historic droughts and famines in Anatolia. Ranging from newspaper cuttings and poems to fossil leaves, tree rings, and timber fragments, the artefacts refer to climate information from the times when meteorological data was not yet systematically collected.
Traces of Escapees (2021) dives into the sea to diagnose the pollution caused by fish farms and the subsequent genetic erosion of local types.
The Lasting Pond (2021) digs into the shrinking of wetlands on the periphery of Istanbul by tracking water buffalos along their wallowing routes.

---

Emirhan Altuner is an Istanbul-based exhibition designer, scenographer and researcher with ten years of experience in cultural institutions. He is the Spatial Design and Production Manager at SALT, where he has been actively involved for the research, design, production, and installation since 2016. Working across both archival material and contemporary art, he develops spatial narratives through temporary structures, graphic systems, and adaptive reuse. Alongside his design practice, he initiated Trash in 2026, the program built from exhibition remnants accumulated over fifteen years of SALT's history, accompanied by a series of talks and workshops. The project extends his ongoing interest in alternative modes of circulating knowledge and material resources generated through exhibition-making. Holding both bachelor's and master's degrees from the Istanbul Technical University (ITU), Faculty of Architecture, his master's thesis, Aurama: A Kind of Optic Assistant in the Fiction-Space, explores the physical and conceptual production of fictive spaces through film, theater, exhibition, and scenography from cinematographic and theoretical perspectives. He has been teaching at the Architectural Drawing Studio at Istanbul Bilgi University since 2023. He was recognized as one of Turkey's Young Architects Under 40 by The Circle in 2023.

AWARDS AND RECOGNITIONS 2023 · The Circle, Istanbul GEMSS: Emerging Architects Selection and Exhibition. 2015 · Istanbul Association of Architects in Private Practice S.O.S. Haydarpaşa Competition — Honorable Mention for project Re-Time [with Pelin Arabacıoğlu]. 2015 · LIXIL Foundation, Japan 5th International University Architectural Competition / First Phase — 3rd Prize for project Hanged, A House for Enjoying the Harsh Cold [with Gumwörk]. 2014 · Architecture Education Association, Turkey Project Awards for Architecture Students — Jury's Special Award for project Valley.

Subsoil by artist Onur Gökmen investigates a largely overlooked episode in Turkey’s environmental and institutional history: the radioactive contamination detected in Black Sea tea following the 1986 Chernobyl disaster. Drawing on research conducted by a group of scientists at Middle East Technical University, including the artist’s parents İnci and Ali Gökmen, the exhibition examines the tensions between scientific evidence, public health, media representation, and state narratives. While reports identified radioactive contamination in tea produced in the region, official responses often downplayed its significance, allowing contaminated products to remain in circulation. Tea subsequently emerged as a material witness to an invisible environmental crisis and its lasting place in collective memory. Combining documentary and fictional elements, the exhibition unfolds through three interconnected scenes. A reconstruction of the METU laboratory where contamination was first measured is accompanied by a documentary based on the testimonies of İnci and Ali Gökmen. A television studio installation examines the role of media in shaping public perception of the crisis, while a short film revisits the narratives that obscured its consequences. Behind these staged environments, a third layer of photographs traces the enduring presence of Chernobyl in Turkey. Together, these fragments reveal how invisible forms of environmental damage continue to shape public health, political discourse, and collective memory across generations.

90s-onstage.title: The 90s Onstage
90s-onstage.location: SALT GALATA / SALT BEYOĞLU, Istanbul, Turkey
